source_paper_title: Dynamic Portfolio Selection for Nonlinear Law-Dependent Preferences
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Liang_2025, title={Dynamic Portfolio Selection for Nonlinear Law-Dependent Preferences}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0345}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0345}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Liang, Zongxia and Xia, Jianming and Yuan, Fengyi}, year={2025}, month=june }
context:
Page 15, Section 6 (Remark 6.2(a))

It is worth noting that (6.4) is a two-dimensional QBSDE, whose two components \(\bar Y_1\) and \(\bar Y_2\) are coupled. Two-dimensional QBSDEs are technically much more challenging than the one-dimensional ones, and unless other assumptions are imposed, the well-posedness remains open; see the bibliographical notes in Subsection 7.4 of Zhang (2017).
title: Well-posedness theory for coupled multidimensional quadratic BSDEs in portfolio equilibria
background_latex:
Let 
\((\Omega,\mathcal F,\mathbb F,\mathbb P)\) be a filtered probability space supporting a Brownian motion \(W\) on \([0,T]\), and consider backward stochastic differential equations (BSDEs) for unknown adapted processes \((Y,Z)\), where \(Y=(Y^1,\dots,Y^m)\) is \(\mathbb R^m\)-valued and \(Z=(Z^1,\dots,Z^m)\) with \(Z^i\in\mathbb R^d\). A (multi-dimensional) quadratic BSDE has the form
\[
Y_t = \xi + \int_t^T f(s,Y_s,Z_s)\,ds - \int_t^T Z_s\,dW_s,
\]
where the generator \(f\) has quadratic growth in \(Z\), e.g. \(|f(s,y,z)|\lesssim 1+|z|^2\). In many applications (including equilibrium characterizations in time-inconsistent portfolio selection with nonlinear law-dependent preferences), one obtains *coupled* systems where components \((Y^i,Z^i)\) interact through cross terms in \(Z\), yielding generators that are neither diagonally quadratic nor triangular.

A basic mathematical issue is *well-posedness*: existence and uniqueness of a solution \((Y,Z)\) in a natural solution class such as bounded \(Y\) and BMO-integrable \(Z\) (i.e., \(\int_0^\cdot Z_s\,dW_s\) is a BMO martingale). Current general results for multi-dimensional quadratic BSDEs typically require additional structural assumptions (e.g. diagonally quadratic or triangular generators) or smallness conditions on data.
problem_definition_latex: Develop general existence and uniqueness results for coupled multi-dimensional quadratic BSDE systems with genuinely coupled (cross-term) quadratic generators, beyond special structures such as diagonally quadratic or triangular forms, in a solution class such as \(Y\in L^\infty\) and \(Z\) BMO-integrable.
latex:
Let 
\((\Omega,\mathcal F,\mathbb F,\mathbb P)\) be a filtered probability space supporting a Brownian motion \(W\) on \([0,T]\), and consider backward stochastic differential equations (BSDEs) for unknown adapted processes \((Y,Z)\), where \(Y=(Y^1,\dots,Y^m)\) is \(\mathbb R^m\)-valued and \(Z=(Z^1,\dots,Z^m)\) with \(Z^i\in\mathbb R^d\). A (multi-dimensional) quadratic BSDE has the form
\[
Y_t = \xi + \int_t^T f(s,Y_s,Z_s)\,ds - \int_t^T Z_s\,dW_s,
\]
where the generator \(f\) has quadratic growth in \(Z\), e.g. \(|f(s,y,z)|\lesssim 1+|z|^2\). In many applications (including equilibrium characterizations in time-inconsistent portfolio selection with nonlinear law-dependent preferences), one obtains *coupled* systems where components \((Y^i,Z^i)\) interact through cross terms in \(Z\), yielding generators that are neither diagonally quadratic nor triangular.

A basic mathematical issue is *well-posedness*: existence and uniqueness of a solution \((Y,Z)\) in a natural solution class such as bounded \(Y\) and BMO-integrable \(Z\) (i.e., \(\int_0^\cdot Z_s\,dW_s\) is a BMO martingale). Current general results for multi-dimensional quadratic BSDEs typically require additional structural assumptions (e.g. diagonally quadratic or triangular generators) or smallness conditions on data.

Develop general existence and uniqueness results for coupled multi-dimensional quadratic BSDE systems with genuinely coupled (cross-term) quadratic generators, beyond special structures such as diagonally quadratic or triangular forms, in a solution class such as \(Y\in L^\infty\) and \(Z\) BMO-integrable.
open_problem_quote: It is worth noting that (6.4) is a two-dimensional QBSDE, whose two components \(\bar Y_1\) and \(\bar Y_2\) are coupled. Two-dimensional QBSDEs are technically much more challenging than the one-dimensional ones, and unless other assumptions are imposed, the well-posedness remains open; see the bibliographical notes in Subsection 7.4 of Zhang (2017).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for a general theory guaranteeing existence/uniqueness, not a single falsifiable claim with a finite parameter search. Automated instance search might find pathological examples, but that would not resolve the well-posedness gap in either direction without substantial theoretical interpretation.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary coding loop could explore families of coupled quadratic generators and numerically solve the associated BSDEs to conjecture sharp smallness/structure conditions (e.g., bounds on BMO norms or monotonicity constants). However, the core contribution sought is a rigorous general theorem, and numerical fitness signals would only provide partial guidance.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem, as formulated in Liang–Xia–Yuan (2023), asks for a general well-posedness theory (existence and uniqueness, typically in \(Y\in L^\infty\) and \(Z\) BMO) for genuinely coupled multidimensional quadratic BSDE systems whose generators contain cross-term quadratic interactions in \(Z\) and do not fall into diagonally quadratic or triangular classes. In their weighted-utility example, the equilibrium first-order condition leads to a coupled 2D QBSDE system (equations (6.3)–(6.4)) and the authors obtain solvability only by exploiting special linear–quadratic structure plus a smallness condition expressed via \(V(\Theta)=\sup_\tau\|\Theta-\mathbb E_\tau[\Theta]\|_\infty\) for \(\Theta=\int_0^T|\kappa_s|^2ds\), i.e., \(\kappa\) close to deterministic. This highlights that the general coupled case remains outside current theory.

Among the forward citations provided, the most directly aligned is "Equilibrium Portfolio Selection under Utility-Variance Analysis of Log Returns in Incomplete Markets", which produces another explicit genuinely coupled 2D quadratic BSDE system for equilibrium controls and similarly shows that existing multidimensional quadratic BSDE results do not apply in the unconstrained correlated-noise regime. It establishes well-posedness only in regimes where the coupling disappears (\(\rho=0\)) or where constraints/projected dynamics bring the system into an AB+BF framework covered by existing results (e.g., Xing–Zitkovi\'c-type conditions), and otherwise resorts to perturbative approximate equilibria for small correlation. The other two citations provide methodological or contextual background (deterministic integral-equation characterization; linear/1D BSDE equilibrium conditions) but do not advance the general \(L^\infty\times\)BMO well-posedness theory for cross-coupled quadratic generators.

Overall, the problem remains open: current progress is application-driven and relies on special structure (pure linear–quadratic forms, measure-change/BMO norm equivalences) and/or smallness assumptions on data, rather than a general existence/uniqueness theorem for fully coupled multidimensional quadratic BSDEs with cross terms. Promising directions suggested by these works include: identifying new structural conditions beyond diagonal/triangular (e.g., monotonicity/convexity in suitable directions, or Lyapunov-function criteria), developing robust a priori BMO estimates stable under coupling, and extending AB/wAB-type frameworks to handle genuine cross-quadratic interactions without smallness constraints.

sources.title: Equilibrium Portfolio Selection under Utility-Variance Analysis of Log Returns in Incomplete Markets
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper derives a coupled 2D quadratic BSDE system characterizing open-loop time-consistent equilibrium strategies in an incomplete factor model with correlated Brownian motions (Theorem 3.1, BSDE (3.1) coupled with feedback law (3.2)). It proves existence/verification of an equilibrium only in special cases: (i) when the Brownian motions are independent, \(\rho=0\), the system decouples and admits a unique bounded solution \((Y,Z)\in S^\infty\times L^2\) (Proposition 4.3) and yields an equilibrium strategy (Theorem 4.4); (ii) under bounded trading constraints \(\pi_t\in A\), the resulting projected-coupled BSDE (4.11) satisfies AB+BF conditions and hence admits a bounded BMO solution by Xing–Zitkovi\'c (2018), which verifies an equilibrium (Theorem 4.11). For the unconstrained correlated case \(\rho\neq 0\), it constructs a strategy \(\pi^\rho\) of the \(\rho=0\) form and proves it is an approximate equilibrium with error \(O(\rho^2)\) (Theorem 5.6), using a PDE/Feynman–Kac approach to obtain bounded \(Y^\rho,Z^\rho\) (Proposition 5.5).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for general well-posedness (existence/uniqueness in \(L^\infty\times\)BMO) for genuinely coupled multidimensional quadratic BSDEs with cross-term quadratic generators, beyond diagonal/triangular structures and smallness assumptions. This paper provides an explicit example of such a genuinely coupled quadratic BSDE system arising from equilibrium (cross-coupling through \(\rho Z\) and the feedback \(\hat\pi\) depending on \(Z,\tilde Z\)), and it explains why standard multidimensional quadratic BSDE theories (contraction/fixed point; AB/wAB-based approximation) do not apply in the general \(\rho\neq 0\) unconstrained case (Appendix C). It then establishes well-posedness only in special regimes: \(\rho=0\) (decoupling removes cross terms) and bounded-control constraint (projection yields a system fitting AB+BF, giving existence of a bounded BMO solution via existing theory). For \(\rho\neq 0\) without constraints, it does not prove existence/uniqueness; instead it introduces an approximate equilibrium with error \(O(\rho^2)\), which is a perturbative/approximation result rather than a general well-posedness theorem.

sources.title: An integral equation in portfolio selection with time-inconsistent preferences
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves existence and uniqueness in 
\(L^2((0,T);\mathbb R^d)\) for a deterministic nonlinear integral equation characterizing equilibrium portfolio weights:
\[a(t)=P_t\Big(h\big(t,\sqrt{\int_t^T|a(s)|^2ds},\int_t^T a(s)^\top\lambda(s)ds\big)\,\lambda(t)\Big),\quad t\in(0,T),\]
where \(\lambda\in L^2((0,T);\mathbb R^d)\), \(P_t\) is projection onto a closed convex set \(U_t\), and \(h\) is locally Lipschitz plus an integrability/boundedness condition (I-boundedness). In particular, if \(h\) is I-bounded and locally Lipschitz, then (Theorem 2.7) the equation has a unique solution \(a\in L^2((0,T);\mathbb R^d)\); local existence holds under local I-boundedness (Lemma 2.6), and uniqueness holds under local Lipschitz (Theorem 2.5).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a well-posedness theory for genuinely coupled multidimensional quadratic BSDE systems (existence/uniqueness with bounded \(Y\) and BMO \(Z\)) arising in portfolio equilibria. This paper does not study BSDEs, nor multidimensional quadratic generators with cross terms in \(Z\). Instead, it bypasses BSDE analysis by reducing certain equilibrium conditions (in a deterministic-coefficient, Brownian market) to a deterministic nonlinear integral equation for a function \(a(t)\) (portfolio weights in volatility units). The fixed-point/contraction arguments and a priori bounds (e.g., Lemma 4.1) are conceptually related to well-posedness questions, but they do not provide existence/uniqueness results for coupled quadratic BSDEs in the \(Y\in L^\infty\), \(Z\in\)BMO class.

sources.title: Time-consistent portfolio selection with strictly monotone mean-variance preference
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper characterizes Nash equilibrium (time-consistent) portfolio controls for strictly monotone mean-variance (SMMV) preferences. For open-loop equilibria it derives a flow of FBSDEs with a linear BSDE and an equilibrium condition on the diagonal: if \((Y^t(\lambda),\bar Y^t(\lambda))\) solves the linear BSDE \[Y_s^{(\lambda)}=\zeta+\theta(\lambda-X_T^{\bar\pi}-\zeta/\theta)_+ +\int_s^T r_v Y_v^{(\lambda)}\,dv-\int_s^T \bar Y_v^{(\lambda)}\,dW_v,\] then \(\bar\pi\) is an open-loop Nash equilibrium iff \(Y_t^t\vartheta_t+\bar Y_t^t=0\) a.s. a.e. \(t\). Under deterministic parameters it gives an explicit equilibrium \(\bar\pi_t=\frac{\beta_t\vartheta_t}{\theta\sigma_t}e^{-\int_t^T r_v dv}\) where \(\beta\ge1\) solves an integral equation (24), and shows the closed-loop equilibrium coincides with this control.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for general well-posedness (existence/uniqueness) theory for genuinely coupled multidimensional quadratic BSDE systems (bounded \(Y\), BMO \(Z\)) arising in portfolio equilibria, beyond diagonal/triangular structures. This paper’s BSDEs are one-dimensional and linear (not multidimensional, not quadratic in \(Z\)), and the main technical objects are a linear BSDE plus an equilibrium condition, and a BSPDE with an indicator terminal condition. It does not develop existence/uniqueness theory for coupled multidimensional quadratic BSDEs with cross-term quadratic generators, nor address BMO well-posedness for such systems. At most it provides application context (equilibrium characterization via BSDE/BSPDE) in time-consistent portfolio selection with law-dependent preferences.
